nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving and still, oxygen-poor streams, swamps, and hill-forest drainages in the Rakhine Yoma (Arakan) hill range; densely vegetated, heavily shaded, leaf-littered waters with soft, acidic, dark-stained conditions

decorPreferences: tight-fitting lid (mandatory — obligate air-breather and active jumper)
dense surface and emergent vegetation (Amazon frogbit, water sprite, floating plants)
driftwood, roots, and leaf litter for cover and tannin release
dim lighting or floating-plant canopy to reduce light intensity
caves or PVC pipe hides for retreat
minimal or no current — still or very slow flow preferred

diet.notes: Piscivore-leaning predator. Wild diet consists of fish, frogs, invertebrates, and other animal prey. In captivity, accepts high-quality meaty frozen foods: whole silversides, lance fish, shrimp, earthworms, mussel, and cockle. Pellet acceptance is possible with patient training using the stick-feeding method, but many individuals remain reluctant. Live feeder fish should be avoided to prevent disease introduction and to discourage exclusive live-prey fixation. Feed every 2–3 days; avoid overfeeding — snakeheads are prone to obesity in captivity.

goodWith: conspecific pair (only with very large tank, 400+ L, extensive cover, and careful monitoring)
robust similarly-sized fish in very large tanks only (not generally recommended)
large barbs or catfish as dither fish in specialist setups
avoidWith: any fish small enough to be swallowed (all will be eaten)
invertebrates (shrimp and snails will be predated)

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Channa pulchra; verify availability and licensing before use.

summary: A striking dwarf snakehead endemic to Myanmar’s Rakhine Yoma hills, described in 2006 by Ralf Britz. Reaches ~30 cm; cool-tolerant (18–26°C); multiple regional color forms known. Popular in the advanced freshwater hobby but legally restricted in the US under the Lacey Act.
careNotes:
Channa pulchra is a dwarf snakehead described in 2006 by ichthyologist Ralf Britz from the Rakhine Yoma (Arakan) hill range of western Myanmar, where it inhabits cool, heavily shaded, slow-moving forest streams. It is one of the smaller Channa species, typically reaching 25–30 cm, and is highly sought after in the advanced freshwater hobby for its striking iridescent blue-green body patterning and orange-edged fins. Several regional color forms exist, including the ‘Yellow Sentarum’ variant, and it is frequently confused with the closely related C. ornatipinnis; reliable identification requires examination of scale counts and fin-ray formula.

Housing requires a species tank of at least 200 L with a minimum 120 cm footprint. A very tight-fitting lid with no gaps is absolutely mandatory — all snakeheads are powerful jumpers and will escape any unsecured opening. Dense floating vegetation, driftwood, and leaf litter replicate the natural blackwater environment and are strongly recommended. Lighting should be subdued; bright light stresses the fish and suppresses natural behavior. Water should be soft, slightly acidic, and maintained at 18–26°C; this species is notably cool-tolerant compared to many other Channa, tolerating temperatures down to 18°C (some sources suggest brief tolerance below 15°C), making supplemental heating optional in temperate climates in summer.

Channa pulchra is an obligate air-breather using a suprabranchial organ and must have unobstructed access to the water surface at all times. Aggressive towards conspecifics and all smaller tankmates; solitary housing is standard practice. A mated pair can be attempted only in a very large, heavily decorated tank with separate feeding stations and close monitoring for aggression. Water quality should be maintained with regular partial water changes; snakeheads are sensitive to ammonia and nitrite accumulation despite their reputation for hardiness.

Note: All Channa species are listed as Injurious Wildlife under the US Lacey Act (18 U.S.C. § 42). Import into and interstate transport within the United States is prohibited without a federal permit. Prospective keepers must verify local and national regulations before acquisition.
breedingNotes: Channa pulchra has been bred in captivity by specialist keepers. Breeding is typically triggered by a cool-down period (dropping temperature to ~18–20°C for several weeks) followed by a gradual warming, mimicking seasonal variation in the native habitat. The species is a biparental substrate or open-water spawner that exhibits active parental care — both parents guard the egg mass and subsequently herd and protect free-swimming fry. Fry are initially fed live Artemia nauplii and micro-worms, progressing to chopped frozen foods as they grow. Separating fry from parents once independent feeding is established is advisable to prevent predation. Breeding difficulty is rated advanced due to conditioning requirements, pairing compatibility challenges, and the specialist-level care required throughout.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Channa pulchra
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided original description (Britz 2006), native range (Rakhine Yoma, Myanmar), water parameters, adult size, diet, tank requirements, behavior notes, and breeding information.

additional.name: FishBase — Channa pulchra
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Channidae, Anabantiformes), adult size, native range (Myanmar endemic), temperature tolerance, and diet (carnivore/piscivore). Confirmed described by Britz 2006.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Snakehead Fish Care
additional.notes: Hobby-oriented care overview covering snakehead husbandry, tank size, feeding, escape risk, and US Lacey Act legal status. Supplemented care notes and legal advisory.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Channa pulchra profile
additional.notes: UK hobbyist source covering color forms (including Yellow Sentarum variant), confusion with C. ornatipinnis, cool-tolerance, and breeding in captivity. Supplemented color form and identification notes.

additional.name: Britz, R. (2006) — Original description via FishBase reference
additional.notes: Original taxonomic description: Britz, R. 2006. Channa pulchra, a new species of snakehead from Myanmar (Teleostei: Channidae). Ichthyological Exploration of Freshwaters 17(1):71–76. Confirms species identity, type locality (Rakhine Yoma), and distinguishing characters from C. ornatipinnis.

fish.breedingNotes: Biparental substrate-spawner with active fry guarding. Breeding triggered by cool-down then warming to simulate seasonal cues. Fry require live Artemia nauplii initially. Advanced difficulty due to pairing compatibility, conditioning, and specialist care demands.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: All Channa species are USDA-listed Injurious Wildlife under the Lacey Act; import and interstate transport banned in the US.
